content:
# Genesis Chapter 5 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3v–4r | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_005_page_001.jpg, GEN_005_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** This is the book of the generations of Adam. On the day that God created man, in the likeness of God He made him.

**2** Male and female He created them, and He blessed them and called their name Adam on the day they were created.

**3** And Adam lived one hundred and thirty years and fathered a son in his own likeness, according to his image, and called his name Seth.

**4** And the days of Adam after he fathered Seth were eight hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**5** And all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years, and he died.

**6** And Seth lived one hundred and five years and fathered Enosh.

**7** And Seth lived after he fathered Enosh eight hundred and seven years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**8** And all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years, and he died.

**9** And Enosh lived ninety years and fathered Kenan.

**10** And Enosh lived after he fathered Kenan eight hundred and fifteen years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**11** And all the days of Enosh were nine hundred and five years, and he died.

**12** And Kenan lived seventy years and fathered Mahalalel.

**13** And Kenan lived after he fathered Mahalalel eight hundred and forty years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**14** And all the days of Kenan were nine hundred and ten years, and he died.

**15** And Mahalalel lived sixty-five years and fathered Jared.

**16** And Mahalalel lived after he fathered Jared eight hundred and thirty years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**17** And all the days of Mahalalel were eight hundred and ninety-five years, and he died.

**18** And Jared lived one hundred and sixty-two years and fathered Enoch.

**19** And Jared lived after he fathered Enoch eight hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**20** And all the days of Jared were nine hundred and sixty-two years, and he died.

**21** And Enoch lived sixty-five years and fathered Methuselah.

**22** And Enoch walked with God after he fathered Methuselah three hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**23** And all the days of Enoch were three hundred and sixty-five years.

**24** And Enoch walked with God, and he was not, for God took him.

**25** And Methuselah lived one hundred and eighty-seven years and fathered Lamech.

**26** And Methuselah lived after he fathered Lamech seven hundred and eighty-two years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**27** And all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred and sixty-nine years, and he died.

**28** And Lamech lived one hundred and eighty-two years and fathered a son.

**29** And he called his name Noah, saying, "This one will bring us relief from our work and from the toil of our hands from the ground, which the LORD has cursed."

**30** And Lamech lived after he fathered Noah five hundred and ninety-five years, and he fathered sons and daughters.

**31** And all the days of Lamech were seven hundred and seventy-seven years, and he died.

**32** And Noah was five hundred years old, and Noah fathered Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of three manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 5 spans Folios 3v–4r. Folio 3v (page 001) is a right-hand page showing three columns of Tiberian vocalized text; the folio contains the conclusion of Genesis 4 in its upper section and the opening of the Genesis 5 genealogy in the lower portion — consistent with the overlap noted in the GEN_004 translation notes, which observed that folio 3v carries through to Genesis 5. Folio 4r (page 002) continues the genealogy through its conclusion at verse 32 and into the opening of Genesis 6. The text was read directly from both manuscript images. The characteristic Tiberian vocalization with full qamets, patah, and shewa pointing is present and legible throughout. Parashah markings appear in the margins; the pe-parashah break at the beginning of Genesis 5 is clearly visible, marking the toledot formula as a new literary unit. The divine name אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, God) is used exclusively throughout this chapter — יהוה (the LORD) does not appear until verse 29, and even there only in the liturgical speech of Lamech.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 5 was read directly. The Greek text is written in the characteristic three-column uncial hand. Genesis 5 in the Vaticanus presents the most sustained and systematic numerical divergence from the Masoretic Text of any chapter in Genesis, documented in full below. The LXX ages are consistently higher than the MT for pre-firstborn lifespans, by approximately one hundred years per patriarch, with Methuselah presenting an exceptional problem noted below. The Vaticanus text is complete for this chapter with no lacunae visible in the image.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. סֵפֶר תּוֹלְדֹת אָדָם (sefer toledot 'adam) — "the book of the generations of Adam"** (v. 1)

The opening formula of Genesis 5 is the second occurrence of the תּוֹלְדֹת (toledot) structural formula in Genesis. The first occurred at 2:4 — אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת הַשָּׁמַיִם וְהָאָרֶץ ("These are the generations of the heavens and the earth") — and marked the beginning of the Eden narrative. Here the formula takes a unique form: it is prefaced with סֵפֶר (sefer, "book, scroll, document"). No other toledot formula in Genesis uses this word. The phrase סֵפֶר תּוֹלְדֹת is a document marker: "This is the document of the generations of Adam."

The word תּוֹלְדֹת (toledot) derives from the root ילד (yalad, "to beget, to bear") and denotes that which is generated, produced, or brought forth — the progeny, the account of begettings, the record of descendants. "Generations" is the standard English rendering (KJV, ESV, NASB) and is the most formally precise: it preserves both the genealogical content (a list of who begot whom) and the wider narrative function of the formula (marking literary units). "Account" (NIV) is smoother but loses the genealogical specificity. "Generations" is retained throughout for formal consistency with all occurrences of the toledot formula in Genesis.

The phrase זֶה סֵפֶר תּוֹלְדֹת אָדָם — "This is the book of the generations of Adam" — has been interpreted as an embedded citation formula: the narrator may be invoking an earlier written source, a putative "Book of the Generations of Adam." Whether such a document existed independently, was a convention of ancient Near Eastern king-list literature, or is a literary device of the Genesis narrator cannot be determined from the text alone. The formal translation renders it as written without adjudicating its source-critical implications.

**2. אָדָם / adam — The transition from generic to proper** (v. 2)

Verse 2 reads: וַיִּבְרָא אֹתָם זָכָר וּנְקֵבָה וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם וַיִּקְרָא אֶת שְׁמָם אָדָם — "Male and female He created them, and He blessed them, and He called their name Adam on the day they were created." The call back to Genesis 1:27 is verbatim: "male and female He created them" is identical. But the name-giving — "He called their name Adam" — is unusual: the name אָדָם (adam) is given to both of them collectively. This reflects the usage of אָדָם in Genesis 1:26–28, where it is a generic term for the human being(s) created in God's image, both male and female together constituting adam.

By verse 3, however, the chapter shifts: "And Adam lived one hundred and thirty years" — here adam has become a personal name (with the definite article dropped and the reference clearly individual and male). The translation must navigate this transition. Verse 1 uses "man" (generic) to render the clause "God created man, in the likeness of God He made him" — preserving the continuity with Genesis 1:26 ("let us make man, adam"). Verse 2 uses "Adam" as the collective name because the naming act itself requires a name to appear in the text. From verse 3 onward, "Adam" is the personal name of the first patriarch.

**3. בִּדְמוּתוֹ כְּצַלְמוֹ (bid'muto ke-tsalmo) — "in his own likeness, according to his image"** (v. 3)

This phrase is the reverse echo of 1:26. At 1:26, God said: נַעֲשֶׂה אָדָם בְּצַלְמֵנוּ כִּדְמוּתֵנוּ — "Let us make man in our image (tselem), according to our likeness (demut)." The word order is tselem first, then demut. At 5:3, Adam fathers Seth בִּדְמוּתוֹ כְּצַלְמוֹ — demut first, then tselem. The inversion is precise and almost certainly deliberate: Adam, who was made in God's tselem-demut, now generates Seth in his own demut-tselem. The image-and-likeness of God has been transmitted — but the order of the terms is reversed, and the possessive is now "his own" rather than God's "ours." The relationship between the divine image-giving (1:26) and the human image-transmitting (5:3) is one of the most discussed themes in the entire Genesis prologue.

The formal translation renders: "in his own likeness, according to his image" — preserving both terms, preserving their reversed order, and using "his own" (not merely "his") to accent the reflexive character of the transmission. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this κατὰ τὴν ἰδέαν αὐτοῦ καὶ κατὰ τὴν εἰκόνα αὐτοῦ — "according to his form and according to his image" — using ἰδέα for demut and εἰκών for tselem. The LXX also reverses the order relative to Genesis 1:26 (which has εἰκών-ὁμοίωσις), confirming that the order reversal in MT 5:3 versus 1:26 is original.

**4. The Genealogical Formula — Preserving the Literary Architecture**

The genealogy of Genesis 5 is constructed from a rigidly repeated four-part formula applied to each patriarch (with variations at Enoch and Noah):

Part A: "And [NAME] lived [X] years and fathered [NEXT NAME]."
Part B: "And [NAME] lived after he fathered [NEXT NAME] [Y] years, and he fathered sons and daughters."
Part C: "And all the days of [NAME] were [Z] years, and he died."
(With Enoch: Part C omits "and he died" and substitutes the walking-with-God narrative.)
(With Noah: Parts A–C are compressed; the formula is partially suspended.)

This repetition is not stylistic monotony — it is literary architecture. The formula functions as a tolling bell: ten times the cycle turns, and at the end of nearly every cycle comes the phrase וַיָּמֹת (vayyamot, "and he died"). The cumulative effect of nine deaths against one exception (Enoch) is the literary point. The formal translation preserves the formulaic repetition verbatim and does not vary the phrasing from patriarch to patriarch. Every instance of "and he fathered sons and daughters" is rendered identically; every "and all the days of" follows the same English structure; every "and he died" lands at the same position in the same words. To vary this phrasing for readability would be to destroy the architecture the Hebrew is building.

**5. הִתְהַלֵּךְ אֶת הָאֱלֹהִים (hithallek et ha-Elohim) — "walked with God"** (vv. 22, 24)

The verb הִתְהַלֵּךְ (hithallek) is the Hithpael (reflexive/iterative) of הלך (halak, "to walk"). The Hithpael form carries the sense of habitual, ongoing, or continuous action: not a single walk but a manner of life, a sustained way of moving through existence. "Walked with God" is the standard formal rendering (KJV, ESV, NASB, JPS). "Walked faithfully with God" (NIV) makes explicit what "with God" leaves implicit and loses the formal starkness of the Hebrew. "Walked" is retained.

The preposition is אֶת (et) — here unambiguously the preposition "with," not the direct object marker (contrast the ambiguous אֶת at 4:1). "With God" (עִם or אֶת in this sense) denotes companionship, relational proximity, cohabitation of the same path. The phrase is applied to Enoch twice (vv. 22 and 24), and appears again in 6:9 of Noah: נֹחַ אֶת הָאֱלֹהִים הִתְהַלֶּךְ נֹחַ ("Noah walked with God"). The phrase establishes a category of righteous companionship with God that is rare in the Genesis narrative.

**6. וְאֵינֶנּוּ כִּי לָקַח אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים (ve-einennu ki laqach oto Elohim) — "and he was not, for God took him"** (v. 24)

This is one of the most striking verses in all of Genesis. The verb לָקַח (laqach, "to take") is the same ordinary verb used throughout the narrative for picking up, taking, marrying, and acquiring. It is not a technical term for translation or assumption. And yet here, applied to Enoch's departure from the scene of human history, it carries everything: God took him. The text offers no further explanation. No chariot of fire (that is Elijah's departure in 2 Kings 2:11), no mountain of vision, no account of what taking meant or where Enoch went. Only: he walked with God, and he was not, for God took him.

The word וְאֵינֶנּוּ (ve-einennu) is a negative existential: "and he was not" or "and he is no more" — the Hebrew construct אֵין (ayin, "there is not") plus the third-person masculine singular suffix. The phrase "he was not" is formally exact. "He was no more" (NIV, NRSV) is equally valid and perhaps more natural in English, but "was not" preserves the stark grammatical form of the Hebrew — the simple negation of existence — rather than softening it into "no more." The translation retains "he was not."

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this: καὶ οὐχ εὑρίσκετο, ὅτι μετέθηκεν αὐτὸν ὁ θεός — "and he was not found, for God translated/transferred him." The LXX makes two significant interpretive moves: (1) it renders וְאֵינֶנּוּ with "was not found" (εὑρίσκετο, imperfect passive of εὑρίσκω, "to find") — implying that people looked for him and could not find him, rather than simply asserting his non-existence; and (2) it uses μετατίθημι ("to transfer, to translate, to move from one place to another") for לָקַח ("to take"), a word that became the technical Greek term for bodily assumption (cf. Heb 11:5, which quotes the LXX directly: μεμετάθηκεν αὐτὸν ὁ θεός, "God translated/transferred him," citing Gen 5:24). The LXX interpretation of Enoch's departure as a bodily translatio — a transfer to another sphere of existence — shaped the entire Jewish and Christian tradition of speculation about Enoch, producing the rich Enochic literature (1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, 3 Enoch). The MT's language is more restrained: God took him. The translation follows the MT.

**7. נֹחַ / נוֹחַ — Noah and the comfort-wordplay** (v. 29)

Verse 29 contains the only occurrence of the divine name יהוה in Genesis 5, embedded in Lamech's speech: "This one will bring us relief (יְנַחֲמֵנוּ, yenachamenu) from our work and from the toil of our hands from the ground, which the LORD has cursed." The name נֹחַ (Noah) is here connected by Lamech to the root נחם (nacham, "to comfort, to console, to relieve") — specifically the Piel stem יְנַחֲמֵנוּ, "he will comfort/relieve us." This is an instance of paronomasia (wordplay on similar sounds): Noah (נֹחַ) sounds like yenachamenu (יְנַחֲמֵנוּ). The roots are not identical — נֹחַ is connected to the root נוח (nuach, "to rest, to settle"), not directly to נחם (nacham, "to comfort") — but the acoustic resonance is real and deliberate.

The translation renders יְנַחֲמֵנוּ as "bring us relief" rather than "comfort us" in order to honor the ambiguity between the rest-root (nuach) and the comfort-root (nacham). "Relief" bridges both: it speaks of rest from burden (nuach) and of consolation from pain (nacham). "Comfort" (KJV) is too purely emotional; "rest" (some interpreters) is too purely physical. "Relief" holds both. The wordplay on the name Noah cannot be reproduced in English and is noted here rather than forced into the text.

**8. Transliteration of Proper Names**

All patriarchal names are rendered in their conventional English spellings as established in the KJV tradition and carried forward in virtually all modern translations. The Hebrew forms are given below for reference:

- אָדָם (Adam)
- שֵׁת (Seth)
- אֱנוֹשׁ (Enosh)
- קֵינָן (Kenan)
- מַהֲלַלְאֵל (Mahalalel — "praise of God")
- יָרֶד (Jared)
- חֲנוֹךְ (Enoch — "dedicated, initiated")
- מְתוּשֶׁלַח (Methuselah — possibly "man of the javelin" or "his death shall bring," the latter connecting to the flood chronology)
- לֶמֶךְ (Lamech)
- נֹחַ (Noah — "rest")
- שֵׁם חָם וָיָפֶת (Shem, Ham, and Japheth)

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 5: The Chronological Divergence

The most sustained divergence between the Masoretic Text (MT) and the LXX in the entire book of Genesis occurs in the numerical data of Genesis 5. The Vaticanus manuscript was read directly for this chapter. The divergence is systematic: the LXX consistently adds approximately 100 years to the age of each patriarch at the birth of his firstborn son (the "pre-firstborn age"), while reducing the remaining years proportionally so that the total lifespan of most patriarchs remains approximately the same or varies only slightly. The net effect is that the LXX places each birth approximately 100 years later, systematically pushing back the chronological framework of the pre-flood world.

Below is a full comparison table of the MT (Leningrad Codex) ages against the LXX (Vaticanus) ages. Where the Samaritan Pentateuch (SP) differs from both, it is noted.

#### Chronological Comparison Table — Genesis 5

| Patriarch | MT: Age at Firstborn | MT: Remaining Years | MT: Total | LXX: Age at Firstborn | LXX: Remaining Years | LXX: Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adam | 130 | 800 | 930 | 230 | 700 | 930 |
| Seth | 105 | 807 | 912 | 205 | 707 | 912 |
| Enosh | 90 | 815 | 905 | 190 | 715 | 905 |
| Kenan | 70 | 840 | 910 | 170 | 740 | 910 |
| Mahalalel | 65 | 830 | 895 | 165 | 730 | 895 |
| Jared | 162 | 800 | 962 | 162 | 800 | 962 |
| Enoch | 65 | 300 | 365 | 165 | 200 | 365 |
| Methuselah | 187 | 782 | 969 | 167 | 802 | **969** |
| Lamech | 182 | 595 | 777 | 188 | 565 | 753 |
| Noah (at flood) | 500 (sons) | — | — | 500 (sons) | — | — |

**The Methuselah Problem:** The LXX chronology creates a serious internal inconsistency at Methuselah. In the MT, Methuselah's chronology is internally consistent: he dies in the year of the flood (969 years after his birth, which by the cumulative MT chronology falls in the flood year — this is the basis for the traditional rabbinic observation that Methuselah died in the year of the flood). In the LXX, however, the numbers as given in Vaticanus — 167 years at Enoch's birth, then 802 remaining years — produce a total of 969 years for Methuselah. But the cumulative LXX chronology places the flood approximately 14 years after Methuselah's death by his own lifespan numbers. Methuselah, under the LXX system as given in Vaticanus, would have died 14 years after the flood. This is an acknowledged inconsistency in the LXX tradition, visible in Vaticanus, and has been the subject of extensive text-critical discussion. Some LXX manuscripts give 187 for Methuselah's pre-firstborn age (matching the MT) rather than 167, which resolves the problem. Vaticanus reads 167. The Samaritan Pentateuch gives different numbers again (67 years at Enoch's birth), producing yet a third chronological scheme. The MT numbers are followed in the translation.

**Why does the LXX diverge?** Three principal explanations have been proposed by scholars:

1. **Independent textual tradition:** The LXX translator worked from a Hebrew Vorlage (underlying text) that already had the higher numbers, representing a different textual tradition from the proto-MT. On this view, the MT and LXX both faithfully represent different ancient textual traditions, neither being a secondary correction of the other.

2. **Deliberate LXX expansion:** The LXX translator (or an editor in the transmission history) added approximately 100 years to each pre-firstborn age for theological or chronological reasons — perhaps to harmonize with other chronological data, to make the patriarchal ages more internally consistent with the post-flood chronology in Genesis 11, or to push the chronological framework further from the Babylonian king-list traditions which the Genesis genealogy structurally resembles.

3. **Deliberate MT reduction:** The proto-MT tradition reduced the pre-firstborn ages (removing approximately 100 years from each) in order to prevent the genealogical data from being used in messianic calculations, or to distinguish Israelite chronology from the inflated figures of Babylonian antediluvian tradition.

All three proposals have serious scholarly support. The text-critical data does not resolve the question definitively. The MT is followed throughout the translation; the LXX variant chronology is documented in full in this note.

---

### Summary Table — Genesis 5 Manuscript Comparison

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | SP | Translation | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5:1 | זֶה סֵפֶר תּוֹלְדֹת אָדָם | Αὕτη ἡ βίβλος γενέσεως ἀνθρώπων | Agrees with MT | "This is the book of the generations of Adam" | LXX βίβλος γενέσεως = direct parallel to NT "biblos geneseos" (Matt 1:1) |
| 5:2 | וַיִּקְרָא אֶת שְׁמָם אָדָם ("He called their name Adam") | καὶ ἐκάλεσεν τὸ ὄνομα αὐτῶν Αδαμ | Agrees with MT | "called their name Adam" | Collective name for both; formal equivalence retains the unusual "their name Adam" |
| 5:3 | בִּדְמוּתוֹ כְּצַלְמוֹ (demut-tselem) | κατὰ τὴν ἰδέαν αὐτοῦ καὶ κατὰ τὴν εἰκόνα αὐτοῦ | Agrees with MT | "in his own likeness, according to his image" | Reversed order from 1:26 (tselem-demut); LXX also reverses |
| 5:6 | 105 years | 205 years | 105 years | 105 | MT followed |
| 5:9 | 90 years | 190 years | 130 years | 90 | MT followed |
| 5:12 | 70 years | 170 years | 70 years | 70 | MT followed |
| 5:15 | 65 years | 165 years | 65 years | 65 | MT followed |
| 5:21 | 65 years | 165 years | 65 years | 65 | MT followed |
| 5:24 | וְאֵינֶנּוּ כִּי לָקַח אֹתוֹ ("he was not, for He took him") | καὶ οὐχ εὑρίσκετο, ὅτι μετέθηκεν αὐτὸν ὁ θεός ("was not found, for God translated him") | Agrees with MT | "he was not, for God took him" | LXX "translated" (μετατίθημι) became the technical term for bodily assumption; Heb 11:5 cites LXX |
| 5:25 | 187 years | 167 years (Vaticanus) | 67 years | 187 | LXX creates flood-year inconsistency at 167; MT followed |
| 5:29 | יְנַחֲמֵנוּ ("he will comfort/relieve us") | οὗτος διαναπαύσει ἡμᾶς ("this one will give us rest") | Agrees with MT | "bring us relief" | LXX uses ἀναπαύω ("to rest"), emphasizing the nuach/rest root over nacham/comfort |
| 5:32 | שֵׁם חָם וָיָפֶת | Σημ, Χαμ, Ιαφεθ | Agrees with MT | "Shem, Ham, and Japheth" | Order consistent throughout; no variant |

---

### Structural and Theological Note

Genesis 5 is the second toledot unit of the book of Genesis. The toledot formula — זֶה סֵפֶר תּוֹלְדֹת — opens the unit at verse 1 and the unit continues through 6:8, where the next toledot formula ("These are the generations of Noah," 6:9) opens a new section. Genesis 5 is thus not a self-contained chapter in the literary architecture of Genesis; it is the opening and central portion of the second major toledot unit, whose continuation describes the flood (6:9 onward).

The chapter performs a structural hinge function: it joins the Eden-to-Exile narrative of Genesis 2–4 with the flood narrative of Genesis 6–9. It does this by translating the individual Adam of Genesis 2–4 into the genealogical Adam who is the fountainhead of the ten-generation line from Adam to Noah. The chapter thus performs three movements simultaneously:

1. **Theological recapitulation:** Verses 1–2 rehearse the creation of humanity in God's image from Genesis 1:26–27, anchoring the genealogy in the primal creative act. The image of God is not lost with the fall of Genesis 3; it is transmitted forward, from Adam through Seth and onward.

2. **Genealogical descent:** Verses 3–31 execute the ten-generation list with relentless formulaic regularity. The literary weight of the repeated "and he died" (וַיָּמֹת) is cumulative: nine times the bell tolls. Death has entered the human world since Genesis 3, and this chapter is its systematic registry. The one exception — Enoch — is all the more luminous for being surrounded by nine deaths.

3. **Eschatological opening:** Verse 32 and Lamech's speech at verse 29 open toward the future. Noah is named with a promise: "This one will bring us relief." The chapter that has catalogued nine generations of mortality closes with the birth of the one through whom the human line will survive the flood. The note of curse — "the ground which the LORD has cursed" — echoes directly to 3:17, where God cursed the ground for Adam's sake. The curse of the ground is the curse Lamech expects Noah to lift.

The divine name in this chapter is exclusively אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, "God") throughout verses 1–24, with the single appearance of יהוה (the LORD) at verse 29 embedded in Lamech's naming speech. This is the inverse of the pattern in Genesis 2–3, where the compound יהוה אֱלֹהִים predominated. The return to the bare "God" (Elohim) in the genealogy of Genesis 5 gives the chapter the character of a cosmic registry — a divine record of the generations, presided over by the Creator God of Genesis 1 rather than the intimate, covenantal יהוה of the Eden narrative.

The number ten is significant. Genesis 5 presents ten antediluvian patriarchs from Adam to Noah. Genesis 11:10–26 will present ten postdiluvian patriarchs from Shem to Terah. The two ten-generation genealogies bracket the flood narrative and are almost certainly structured in deliberate parallel. Ancient Near Eastern parallels — particularly the Sumerian King List, which also presents ten antediluvian kings with vastly inflated reigns — have been widely noted. The Genesis genealogy uses the same structure (ten figures, enormous lifespans, culminating in a flood) but transforms its content: the patriarchs are not kings but fathers; their significance is not political authority but genealogical descent; and the LORD's purpose is not the legitimacy of dynasty but the survival of the image-bearing human family into the post-flood world.

The formulaic "and he died" tolls nine times: Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared, Methuselah, Lamech, and by implication all the unnamed "sons and daughters." The tenth patriarch — Noah — does not receive this formula in chapter 5. He lives on into the next narrative unit. Enoch alone does not die at all. The two exceptions to the formula — Enoch who was taken and Noah who survives — are the two figures around whom the major narrative movements of Genesis 5–9 are organized.

— LOGOS
